Unit tests alone on the merge helper would miss the state.json and file system coordination. - **Unchanged invariants:** `bunx cairn init` behavior is unchanged for a fresh directory (no new prompts, no new required flags). `doctor` exit-on-error behavior unchanged (schema drift is a warning, not an error). `templates/CAIRN.md` content unchanged by this plan — only archived. ## Risks & Dependencies | Risk | Mitigation | |------|------------| | `git merge-file` not available on the user's machine | Cairn users run `bunx`, which requires Node/Bun; git is a near-universal dependency in this population, but detect missing `git` at sync time and fall back to the degraded 2-way prompt. Surface a one-line note in `doctor` when git is absent so users understand the capability. | | Maintainers forget to archive the previous `templates/CAIRN.md` before bumping `SCHEMA_VERSION` | Add the bump checklist to `templates/versions/README.md`. Consider a future CI check that verifies every integer from `1..SCHEMA_VERSION` has a corresponding `CAIRN-v{N}.md` — out of scope for this plan but noted. | | Conflict-marker output confuses non-git users | Print a clear hint on exit 1: "Conflicts written to CAIRN.md. Resolve markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`) in your editor, then re-run `cairn sync-templates` to finalize." | | User runs `sync-templates` accidentally on a customized vault and overwrites their hand-written CAIRN.md | Always backup; always prompt unless `--yes`; diff preview shown before confirm. Backup filename uses ISO timestamp so multiple runs do not clobber each other. | | Schema bump where conflict-free merge produces a semantically broken vault (e.g., a section renamed the user depended on) | Out of technical scope — this is a release-quality concern for whoever bumps `SCHEMA_VERSION`. Maintainers should note breaking changes in a CHANGELOG entry; `sync-templates` output includes the version span ("reconciling v1 → v2") so users know to read release notes. | ## Documentation / Operational Notes - Update `README.md` install section: add a short note about `cairn sync-templates` for existing users when a plugin upgrade ships a schema bump. Keep it terse — one paragraph under the existing install block. - Add a `## Schema Versioning` subsection to `README.md` or a separate `docs/schema.md` explaining the versioning convention (integer `SCHEMA_VERSION`, archive in `templates/versions/`, maintainer bump checklist). Decide at implementation time which file hosts it. - No migration for the current repo's archived v1 file — the shipping `templates/CAIRN.md` at release time becomes v1. - No telemetry added. ## Sources & References - **Origin:** `docs/ideation/2026-04-17-open-ideation.md` (idea #1: Schema migration & template drift reconciliation) - Related files: `src/lib/constants.ts`, `src/lib/vault.ts`, `src/lib/templates.ts`, `src/commands/init.ts`, `src/commands/doctor.ts`, `src/cli.ts` - Related tests: `tests/init.test.ts` - Prior schema change context: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-15-karpathy-alignment-design.md`, `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-15-v0.3.0-wiki-improvements.md`

10	- The confidence check strengthens rationale, sequencing, risk treatment, and grounding

Headless mode applies auto-fixes silently and returns structured findings without interactive prompts. Address any P0/P1 findings before returning control to the caller.

32	**Pipeline mode:** If invoked from an automated workflow such as LFG, SLFG, or any `disable-model-invocation` context, skip the interactive menu below and return control to the caller immediately. The plan file has already been written, the confidence check has already run, and document-review has already run — the caller (e.g., lfg, slfg) determines the next step.
33	
34	After document-review completes, present the options using the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini). If no question tool is available, present the numbered options in chat and wait for the user's reply before proceeding.
35	
36	**Question:** "Plan ready at `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-NNN-<type>-<name>-plan.md`. What would you like to do next?"
37	
38	**Options:**
39	1. **Start `/ce:work`** (recommended) - Begin implementing this plan in the current session
40	2. **Create Issue** - Create a tracked issue from this plan in your configured issue tracker (GitHub or Linear)
41	3. **Open in Proof (web app) — review and comment to iterate with the agent** - Open the doc in Every's Proof editor, iterate with the agent via comments, or copy a link to share with others
42	4. **Done for now** - Pause; the plan file is saved and can be resumed later
43	
44	**Surface additional document review contextually, not as a menu fixture:** When the prior document-review pass surfaced residual P0/P1 findings that the user has not addressed, mention them adjacent to the menu and offer another review pass in prose (e.g., "Document review flagged 2 P1 findings you may want to address — want me to run another pass before you pick?"). Do not add it to the option list.

46	Based on selection:
47	- **Start `/ce:work`** -> Call `/ce:work` with the plan path
48	- **Create Issue** -> Follow the Issue Creation section below
49	- **Open in Proof (web app) — review and comment to iterate with the agent** -> Load the `proof` skill in HITL-review mode with:

51	  - doc title: `Plan: <plan title from frontmatter>`
52	  - identity: `ai:compound-engineering` / `Compound Engineering`
53	  - recommended next step: `/ce:work` (shown in the proof skill's final terminal output)
54	
55	  Follow `references/hitl-review.md` in the proof skill. It uploads the plan, prompts the user for review in Proof's web UI, ingests each thread by reading it fresh and replying in-thread, applies agreed edits as tracked suggestions, and syncs the final markdown back to the plan file atomically on proceed.
56	
57	  When the proof skill returns:
58	  - `status: proceeded` with `localSynced: true` -> the plan on disk now reflects the review. Re-run `document-review` on the updated plan before re-rendering the menu — HITL can materially rewrite the plan body, so the prior document-review pass no longer covers the current file and section 5.3.8 requires a review before any handoff option is offered. Then return to the post-generation options with the refreshed residual findings.
59	  - `status: proceeded` with `localSynced: false` -> the reviewed version lives in Proof at `docUrl` but the local copy is stale. Offer to pull the Proof doc to `localPath` using the proof skill's Pull workflow. If the pull happened, re-run `document-review` on the pulled file before re-rendering the options (same 5.3.8 rationale — the local plan was materially updated by the pull). If the pull was declined, include a one-line note above the menu that `<localPath>` is stale vs. Proof — otherwise `Start /ce:work` or `Create Issue` will silently use the pre-review copy.
60	  - `status: done_for_now` -> the plan on disk may be stale if the user edited in Proof before leaving. Offer to pull the Proof doc to `localPath` so the local plan file stays in sync. If the pull happened, re-run `document-review` on the pulled file before re-rendering the options (same 5.3.8 rationale). If the pull was declined, include the stale-local note above the menu. `done_for_now` means the user stopped the HITL loop — it does not mean they ended the whole plan session; they may still want to start work or create an issue.
61	  - `status: aborted` -> fall back to the options without changes.
62	
63	  If the initial upload fails (network error, Proof API down), retry once after a short wait. If it still fails, tell the user the upload didn't succeed and briefly explain why, then return to the options — don't leave them wondering why the option did nothing.
64	- **Done for now** -> Display a brief confirmation that the plan file is saved and end the turn
65	- **If the user asks for another document review** (either from the contextual prompt when P0/P1 findings remain, or by free-form request) -> Load the `document-review` skill with the plan path for another pass, then return to the options
66	- **Other** -> Accept free text for revisions and loop back to options
67	
68	## Issue Creation
69	
70	When the user selects "Create Issue", detect their project tracker:
71	
72	1. Read `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md` for compatibility) at the repo root and look for `project_tracker: github` or `project_tracker: linear`.
73	2. If `project_tracker: github`:
74	
75	   ```bash
76	   gh issue create --title "<type>: <title>" --body-file <plan_path>
77	   ```
78	
79	3. If `project_tracker: linear`:
80	
81	   ```bash
82	   linear issue create --title "<title>" --description "$(cat <plan_path>)"
83	   ```

85	4. If no tracker is configured, ask the user which tracker they use with the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini). If no question tool is available, ask in chat and wait for the reply. Options: `GitHub`, `Linear`, `Skip`. Then:
86	   - Proceed with the chosen tracker's command above
87	   - Offer to persist the choice by adding `project_tracker: <value>` to `AGENTS.md`, where `<value>` is the lowercase tracker key (`github` or `linear`) — not the display label — so future runs match the detector in step 1 and skip this prompt
88	   - If `Skip`, return to the options without creating an issue
89	
90	5. If the detected tracker's CLI is not installed or not authenticated, surface a clear error (e.g., "`gh` CLI not found — install it or create the issue manually") and return to the options.

94	- Ask whether to proceed to `/ce:work` using the platform's blocking question tool

# Plan Handoff

This file contains post-plan-writing instructions: document review, post-generation options, and issue creation. Load it after the plan file has been written and the confidence check (5.3.1-5.3.7) is complete.

## 5.3.8 Document Review

After the confidence check (and any deepening), run the `document-review` skill on the plan file. Pass the plan path as the argument. When this step is reached, it is mandatory — do not skip it because the confidence check already ran. The two tools catch different classes of issues.

The confidence check and document-review are complementary:
- The confidence check strengthens rationale, sequencing, risk treatment, and grounding
- Document-review checks coherence, feasibility, scope alignment, and surfaces role-specific issues

If document-review returns findings that were auto-applied, note them briefly when presenting handoff options. If residual P0/P1 findings were surfaced, mention them so the user can decide whether to address them before proceeding.

When document-review returns "Review complete", proceed to Final Checks.

**Pipeline mode:** If invoked from an automated workflow such as LFG, SLFG, or any `disable-model-invocation` context, run `document-review` with `mode:headless` and the plan path. Headless mode applies auto-fixes silently and returns structured findings without interactive prompts. Address any P0/P1 findings before returning control to the caller.

## 5.3.9 Final Checks and Cleanup

Before proceeding to post-generation options:
- Confirm the plan is stronger in specific ways, not merely longer
- Confirm the planning boundary is intact
- Confirm origin decisions were preserved when an origin document exists

If artifact-backed mode was used:
- Clean up the temporary scratch directory after the plan is safely updated
- If cleanup is not practical on the current platform, note where the artifacts were left

## 5.4 Post-Generation Options

**Pipeline mode:** If invoked from an automated workflow such as LFG, SLFG, or any `disable-model-invocation` context, skip the interactive menu below and return control to the caller immediately. The plan file has already been written, the confidence check has already run, and document-review has already run — the caller (e.g., lfg, slfg) determines the next step.

After document-review completes, present the options using the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini). If no question tool is available, present the numbered options in chat and wait for the user's reply before proceeding.

**Question:** "Plan ready at `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-NNN-<type>-<name>-plan.md`. What would you like to do next?"

**Options:**
1. **Start `/ce:work`** (recommended) - Begin implementing this plan in the current session
2. **Create Issue** - Create a tracked issue from this plan in your configured issue tracker (GitHub or Linear)
3. **Open in Proof (web app) — review and comment to iterate with the agent** - Open the doc in Every's Proof editor, iterate with the agent via comments, or copy a link to share with others
4. **Done for now** - Pause; the plan file is saved and can be resumed later

**Surface additional document review contextually, not as a menu fixture:** When the prior document-review pass surfaced residual P0/P1 findings that the user has not addressed, mention them adjacent to the menu and offer another review pass in prose (e.g., "Document review flagged 2 P1 findings you may want to address — want me to run another pass before you pick?"). Do not add it to the option list.

Based on selection:
- **Start `/ce:work`** -> Call `/ce:work` with the plan path
- **Create Issue** -> Follow the Issue Creation section below
- **Open in Proof (web app) — review and comment to iterate with the agent** -> Load the `proof` skill in HITL-review mode with:
  - source file: `docs/plans/<plan_filename>.md`
  - doc title: `Plan: <plan title from frontmatter>`
  - identity: `ai:compound-engineering` / `Compound Engineering`
  - recommended next step: `/ce:work` (shown in the proof skill's final terminal output)

  Follow `references/hitl-review.md` in the proof skill. It uploads the plan, prompts the user for review in Proof's web UI, ingests each thread by reading it fresh and replying in-thread, applies agreed edits as tracked suggestions, and syncs the final markdown back to the plan file atomically on proceed.

  When the proof skill returns:
  - `status: proceeded` with `localSynced: true` -> the plan on disk now reflects the review. Re-run `document-review` on the updated plan before re-rendering the menu — HITL can materially rewrite the plan body, so the prior document-review pass no longer covers the current file and section 5.3.8 requires a review before any handoff option is offered. Then return to the post-generation options with the refreshed residual findings.
  - `status: proceeded` with `localSynced: false` -> the reviewed version lives in Proof at `docUrl` but the local copy is stale. Offer to pull the Proof doc to `localPath` using the proof skill's Pull workflow. If the pull happened, re-run `document-review` on the pulled file before re-rendering the options (same 5.3.8 rationale — the local plan was materially updated by the pull). If the pull was declined, include a one-line note above the menu that `<localPath>` is stale vs. Proof — otherwise `Start /ce:work` or `Create Issue` will silently use the pre-review copy.
  - `status: done_for_now` -> the plan on disk may be stale if the user edited in Proof before leaving. Offer to pull the Proof doc to `localPath` so the local plan file stays in sync. If the pull happened, re-run `document-review` on the pulled file before re-rendering the options (same 5.3.8 rationale). If the pull was declined, include the stale-local note above the menu. `done_for_now` means the user stopped the HITL loop — it does not mean they ended the whole plan session; they may still want to start work or create an issue.
  - `status: aborted` -> fall back to the options without changes.

  If the initial upload fails (network error, Proof API down), retry once after a short wait. If it still fails, tell the user the upload didn't succeed and briefly explain why, then return to the options — don't leave them wondering why the option did nothing.
- **Done for now** -> Display a brief confirmation that the plan file is saved and end the turn
- **If the user asks for another document review** (either from the contextual prompt when P0/P1 findings remain, or by free-form request) -> Load the `document-review` skill with the plan path for another pass, then return to the options
- **Other** -> Accept free text for revisions and loop back to options

## Issue Creation

When the user selects "Create Issue", detect their project tracker:

1. Read `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md` for compatibility) at the repo root and look for `project_tracker: github` or `project_tracker: linear`.
2. If `project_tracker: github`:

   ```bash
   gh issue create --title "<type>: <title>" --body-file <plan_path>
   ```

3. If `project_tracker: linear`:

   ```bash
   linear issue create --title "<title>" --description "$(cat <plan_path>)"
   ```

4. If no tracker is configured, ask the user which tracker they use with the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini). If no question tool is available, ask in chat and wait for the reply. Options: `GitHub`, `Linear`, `Skip`. Then:
   - Proceed with the chosen tracker's command above
   - Offer to persist the choice by adding `project_tracker: <value>` to `AGENTS.md`, where `<value>` is the lowercase tracker key (`github` or `linear`) — not the display label — so future runs match the detector in step 1 and skip this prompt
   - If `Skip`, return to the options without creating an issue

5. If the detected tracker's CLI is not installed or not authenticated, surface a clear error (e.g., "`gh` CLI not found — install it or create the issue manually") and return to the options.

After issue creation:
- Display the issue URL
- Ask whether to proceed to `/ce:work` using the platform's blocking question tool

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/justinbeaudry/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/2.67.0/skills/document-review

# Document Review

Review requirements or plan documents through multi-persona analysis. Dispatches specialized reviewer agents in parallel, auto-fixes quality issues, and presents strategic questions for user decision.

## Phase 0: Detect Mode

Check the skill arguments for `mode:headless`. Arguments may contain a document path, `mode:headless`, or both. Tokens starting with `mode:` are flags, not file paths -- strip them from the arguments and use the remaining token (if any) as the document path for Phase 1.

If `mode:headless` is present, set **headless mode** for the rest of the workflow.

**Headless mode** changes the interaction model, not the classification boundaries. Document-review still applies the same judgment about what has one clear correct fix vs. what needs user judgment. The only difference is how non-auto findings are delivered:
- `auto` fixes are applied silently (same as interactive)
- `present` findings are returned as structured text for the caller to handle -- no AskUserQuestion prompts, no interactive approval
- Phase 5 returns immediately with "Review complete" (no refine/complete question)

The caller receives findings with their original classifications intact and decides what to do with them.

Callers invoke headless mode by including `mode:headless` in the skill arguments, e.g.:
```
Skill("compound-engineering:document-review", "mode:headless docs/plans/my-plan.md")
```


If `mode:headless` is not present, the skill runs in its default interactive mode with no behavior change.

## Phase 1: Get and Analyze Document

**If a document path is provided:** Read it, then proceed.

**If no document is specified (interactive mode):** Ask which document to review, or find the most recent in `docs/brainstorms/` or `docs/plans/` using a file-search/glob tool (e.g., Glob in Claude Code).

**If no document is specified (headless mode):** Output "Review failed: headless mode requires a document path. Re-invoke with: Skill(\"compound-engineering:document-review\", \"mode:headless <path>\")" without dispatching agents.

### Classify Document Type

After reading, classify the document:
- **requirements** -- from `docs/brainstorms/`, focuses on what to build and why
- **plan** -- from `docs/plans/`, focuses on how to build it with implementation details

### Select Conditional Personas

Analyze the document content to determine which conditional personas to activate. Check for these signals:

**product-lens** -- activate when the document makes challengeable claims about what to build and why, or when the proposed work carries strategic weight beyond the immediate problem. The system's users may be end users, developers, operators, maintainers, or any other audience -- the criteria are domain-agnostic. Check for either leg:

*Leg 1 — Premise claims:* The document stakes a position on what to build or why that a knowledgeable stakeholder could reasonably challenge -- not merely describing a task or restating known requirements:
- Problem framing where the stated need is non-obvious or debatable, not self-evident from existing context
- Solution selection where alternatives plausibly exist (implicit or explicit)
- Prioritization decisions that explicitly rank what gets built vs deferred
- Goal statements that predict specific user outcomes, not just restate constraints or describe deliverables

*Leg 2 — Strategic weight:* The proposed work could affect system trajectory, user perception, or competitive positioning, even if the premise is sound:
- Changes that shape how the system is perceived or what it becomes known for
- Complexity or simplicity bets that affect adoption, onboarding, or cognitive load
- Work that opens or closes future directions (path dependencies, architectural commitments)
- Opportunity cost implications -- building this means not building something else

**design-lens** -- activate when the document contains:
- UI/UX references, frontend components, or visual design language
- User flows, wireframes, screen/page/view mentions
- Interaction descriptions (forms, buttons, navigation, modals)
- References to responsive behavior or accessibility

**security-lens** -- activate when the document contains:
- Auth/authorization mentions, login flows, session management
- API endpoints exposed to external clients
- Data handling, PII, payments, tokens, credentials, encryption
- Third-party integrations with trust boundary implications

**scope-guardian** -- activate when the document contains:
- Multiple priority tiers (P0/P1/P2, must-have/should-have/nice-to-have)
- Large requirement count (>8 distinct requirements or implementation units)
- Stretch goals, nice-to-haves, or "future work" sections
- Scope boundary language that seems misaligned with stated goals
- Goals that don't clearly connect to requirements

**adversarial** -- activate when the document contains:
- More than 5 distinct requirements or implementation units
- Explicit architectural or scope decisions with stated rationale
- High-stakes domains (auth, payments, data migrations, external integrations)
- Proposals of new abstractions, frameworks, or significant architectural patterns

## Phase 2: Announce and Dispatch Personas

### Announce the Review Team

Tell the user which personas will review and why. For conditional personas, include the justification:

```
Reviewing with:
- coherence-reviewer (always-on)
- feasibility-reviewer (always-on)
- scope-guardian-reviewer -- plan has 12 requirements across 3 priority levels
- security-lens-reviewer -- plan adds API endpoints with auth flow
```

### Build Agent List

Always include:
- `compound-engineering:document-review:coherence-reviewer`
- `compound-engineering:document-review:feasibility-reviewer`

Add activated conditional personas:
- `compound-engineering:document-review:product-lens-reviewer`
- `compound-engineering:document-review:design-lens-reviewer`
- `compound-engineering:document-review:security-lens-reviewer`
- `compound-engineering:document-review:scope-guardian-reviewer`
- `compound-engineering:document-review:adversarial-document-reviewer`

### Dispatch

Dispatch all agents in **parallel** using the platform's task/agent tool (e.g., Agent tool in Claude Code, spawn in Codex). Omit the `mode` parameter so the user's configured permission settings apply. Each agent receives the prompt built from the subagent template included below with these variables filled:

| Variable | Value |
|----------|-------|
| `{persona_file}` | Full content of the agent's markdown file |
| `{schema}` | Content of the findings schema included below |
| `{document_type}` | "requirements" or "plan" from Phase 1 classification |
| `{document_path}` | Path to the document |
| `{document_content}` | Full text of the document |

Pass each agent the **full document** -- do not split into sections.

**Error handling:** If an agent fails or times out, proceed with findings from agents that completed. Note the failed agent in the Coverage section. Do not block the entire review on a single agent failure.

**Dispatch limit:** Even at maximum (7 agents), use parallel dispatch. These are document reviewers with bounded scope reading a single document -- parallel is safe and fast.

## Phases 3-5: Synthesis, Presentation, and Next Action

After all dispatched agents return, read `references/synthesis-and-presentation.md` for the synthesis pipeline (validate, gate, dedup, promote, resolve contradictions, route by autofix class), auto-fix application, finding presentation, and next-action menu. Do not load this file before agent dispatch completes.

---

## Included References

### Subagent Template

@./references/subagent-template.md

### Findings Schema

@./references/findings-schema.json


ARGUMENTS: docs/plans/2026-04-17-002-feat-template-schema-migration-plan.md

content.content:
1	# Document Review Sub-agent Prompt Template
2	
3	This template is used by the document-review orchestrator to spawn each reviewer sub-agent. Variable substitution slots are filled at dispatch time.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Template
8	
9	```
10	You are a specialist document reviewer.
11	
12	<persona>
13	{persona_file}
14	</persona>
15	
16	<output-contract>
17	Return ONLY valid JSON matching the findings schema below. No prose, no markdown, no explanation outside the JSON object.
18	
19	{schema}
20	
21	Rules:
22	- You are a leaf reviewer inside an already-running compound-engineering review workflow. Do not invoke compound-engineering skills or agents unless this template explicitly instructs you to. Perform your analysis directly and return findings in the required output format only.
23	- Suppress any finding below your stated confidence floor (see your Confidence calibration section).
24	- Every finding MUST include at least one evidence item -- a direct quote from the document.
25	- You are operationally read-only. Analyze the document and produce findings. Do not edit the document, create files, or make changes. You may use non-mutating tools (file reads, glob, grep, git log) to gather context about the codebase when evaluating feasibility or existing patterns.
26	- Set `finding_type` for every finding:
27	  - `error`: Something the document says that is wrong -- contradictions, incorrect statements, design tensions, incoherent tradeoffs.
28	  - `omission`: Something the document forgot to say -- missing mechanical steps, absent list entries, undefined thresholds, forgotten cross-references.
29	- Set `autofix_class` based on whether there is one clear correct fix, not on severity or importance:
30	  - `auto`: One clear correct fix, applied silently. This includes trivial fixes AND substantive ones:
31	    - Internal reconciliation -- one document part authoritative over another (summary/detail mismatches, wrong counts, stale cross-references, terminology drift)
32	    - Implied additions -- correct content mechanically obvious from the document (missing steps, unstated thresholds, completeness gaps)
33	    - Codebase-pattern-resolved -- an established codebase pattern resolves ambiguity (cite the specific file/function in `why_it_matters`)
34	    - Incorrect behavior -- the document describes behavior that is factually wrong, and the correct behavior is obvious from context or the codebase
35	    - Missing standard security measures -- HTTPS enforcement, checksum verification, input sanitization, private IP rejection, or other controls with known implementations where omission is clearly a bug
36	    - Incomplete technical descriptions -- the accurate/complete version is directly derivable from the codebase
37	    - Missing requirements that follow mechanically from the document's own explicit, concrete decisions (not high-level goals -- a goal can be satisfied by multiple valid requirements)
38	    The test is not "is this fix important?" but "is there more than one reasonable way to fix this?" If a competent implementer would arrive at the same fix independently, it is auto -- even if the fix is substantive. Always include `suggested_fix`. NOT auto if more than one reasonable fix exists or if scope/priority judgment is involved.
39	  - `present`: Requires user judgment -- genuinely multiple valid approaches where the right choice depends on priorities, tradeoffs, or context the reviewer does not have. Examples: architectural choices with real tradeoffs, scope decisions, feature prioritization, UX design choices.
40	- `suggested_fix` is required for `auto` findings. For `present` findings, include only when the fix is obvious.
41	- If you find no issues, return an empty findings array. Still populate residual_risks and deferred_questions if applicable.
42	- Use your suppress conditions. Do not flag issues that belong to other personas.
43	</output-contract>
44

48

50	{document_content}

file.content:
# Document Review Sub-agent Prompt Template

This template is used by the document-review orchestrator to spawn each reviewer sub-agent. Variable substitution slots are filled at dispatch time.

---

## Template

```
You are a specialist document reviewer.

<persona>
{persona_file}
</persona>

<output-contract>
Return ONLY valid JSON matching the findings schema below. No prose, no markdown, no explanation outside the JSON object.

{schema}

Rules:
- You are a leaf reviewer inside an already-running compound-engineering review workflow. Do not invoke compound-engineering skills or agents unless this template explicitly instructs you to. Perform your analysis directly and return findings in the required output format only.
- Suppress any finding below your stated confidence floor (see your Confidence calibration section).
- Every finding MUST include at least one evidence item -- a direct quote from the document.
- You are operationally read-only. Analyze the document and produce findings. Do not edit the document, create files, or make changes. You may use non-mutating tools (file reads, glob, grep, git log) to gather context about the codebase when evaluating feasibility or existing patterns.
- Set `finding_type` for every finding:
  - `error`: Something the document says that is wrong -- contradictions, incorrect statements, design tensions, incoherent tradeoffs.
  - `omission`: Something the document forgot to say -- missing mechanical steps, absent list entries, undefined thresholds, forgotten cross-references.
- Set `autofix_class` based on whether there is one clear correct fix, not on severity or importance:
  - `auto`: One clear correct fix, applied silently. This includes trivial fixes AND substantive ones:
    - Internal reconciliation -- one document part authoritative over another (summary/detail mismatches, wrong counts, stale cross-references, terminology drift)
    - Implied additions -- correct content mechanically obvious from the document (missing steps, unstated thresholds, completeness gaps)
    - Codebase-pattern-resolved -- an established codebase pattern resolves ambiguity (cite the specific file/function in `why_it_matters`)
    - Incorrect behavior -- the document describes behavior that is factually wrong, and the correct behavior is obvious from context or the codebase
    - Missing standard security measures -- HTTPS enforcement, checksum verification, input sanitization, private IP rejection, or other controls with known implementations where omission is clearly a bug
    - Incomplete technical descriptions -- the accurate/complete version is directly derivable from the codebase
    - Missing requirements that follow mechanically from the document's own explicit, concrete decisions (not high-level goals -- a goal can be satisfied by multiple valid requirements)
    The test is not "is this fix important?" but "is there more than one reasonable way to fix this?" If a competent implementer would arrive at the same fix independently, it is auto -- even if the fix is substantive. Always include `suggested_fix`. NOT auto if more than one reasonable fix exists or if scope/priority judgment is involved.
  - `present`: Requires user judgment -- genuinely multiple valid approaches where the right choice depends on priorities, tradeoffs, or context the reviewer does not have. Examples: architectural choices with real tradeoffs, scope decisions, feature prioritization, UX design choices.
- `suggested_fix` is required for `auto` findings. For `present` findings, include only when the fix is obvious.
- If you find no issues, return an empty findings array. Still populate residual_risks and deferred_questions if applicable.
- Use your suppress conditions. Do not flag issues that belong to other personas.
</output-contract>

<review-context>
Document type: {document_type}
Document path: {document_path}

Document content:
{document_content}
</review-context>
```

2	  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
3	  "title": "Document Review Findings",

11	    },

15	      "items": {
16	        "type": "object",
17	        "required": [
18	          "title",
19	          "severity",
20	          "section",
21	          "why_it_matters",

26	        ],
27	        "properties": {

31	            "maxLength": 100

36	            "description": "Issue severity level"

Errors are design tensions, contradictions, or incorrect statements. Omissions are missing mechanical steps, forgotten list entries, or absent details."

72	        }

78	      "items": { "type": "string" }
79	    },

81	      "type": "array",

84	    }

Review this plan document for coherence: contradictions between sections, terminology drift, structural issues, ambiguity where readers would diverge.

Document type: plan
Document path: docs/plans/2026-04-17-002-feat-template-schema-migration-plan.md

Return ONLY valid JSON per the document-review findings schema. Every finding needs at least one evidence quote. Set finding_type (error/omission) and autofix_class (auto/present). Suggested_fix required for auto.

Read the file at the given path directly with your file-read tool to get the full content.

Evaluate this plan for feasibility: architecture conflicts, dependency gaps, migration risks, implementability. Check against the real codebase.

Document type: plan

Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/cairn. Relevant files referenced in plan: src/lib/constants.ts, src/lib/vault.ts, src/lib/templates.ts, src/commands/init.ts, src/commands/doctor.ts, src/cli.ts, tests/init.test.ts, templates/CAIRN.md.

Return ONLY valid JSON per the findings schema. Evidence quotes required. Set finding_type and autofix_class. Use Grep/Glob/Read to verify claims against actual code.

Read the plan file directly.

Review this plan as a senior product leader. Challenge premise claims, assess strategic consequences (trajectory, identity, adoption, opportunity cost), surface goal-work misalignment.

Document type: plan (Cairn plugin — markdown knowledge vault for Claude Code; users are developers running `bunx cairn ...` and agents operating on the vault)
Document path: docs/plans/2026-04-17-002-feat-template-schema-migration-plan.md

Key premise under scrutiny: "template drift is a problem worth solving now, and versioning CAIRN.md + a sync command is the right lever." Challenge it.

Return ONLY valid JSON per findings schema. Evidence quotes required.

Read the plan file directly.

Adversarially review this plan. Challenge premises, surface unstated assumptions, stress-test architectural decisions. Don't evaluate document quality — break the plan.

Document type: plan
Document path: docs/plans/2026-04-17-002-feat-template-schema-migration-plan.md

Plan introduces: `SCHEMA_VERSION` integer + `templates/versions/` archive + `cairn sync-templates` command using `git merge-file` for 3-way merge. Backup-then-write, degraded 2-way fallback.

Attack: failure modes of 3-way merge on prose markdown (vs code); `git merge-file` absence/portability; maintainer-forgets-to-archive race; race between concurrent runs; atomicity guarantees; what happens on partial apply; what happens when user edits between backup and write.

Return ONLY valid JSON per findings schema. Evidence quotes required. Read the plan file directly.

Update Unit 4 degraded 2-way path to prompt explicitly: 'Warning: no version history available. This will compare your CAIRN.md to the current template. Edits you made will be shown, but conflicts will require manual resolution or overwrite. Continue? (y/n)' Add test scenario: 'Edge case — schemaVersion absent, vault has custom edits: vault state.json lacks schemaVersion field; CAIRN.md contains user additions; command enters degraded 2-way path, shows plain diff with user changes highlighted, requires explicit confirmation before any overwrite.'"
    },
    {
      "finding_id": "F007",
      "finding_type": "structural",
      "confidence": 0.70,
      "severity": "low",
      "location": "Requirements Trace section",
      "evidence": [
        "Requirements are listed flat: 'R1. Every Cairn vault... R2. cairn doctor... R3. A new cairn sync-templates... R4. Historical template versions... R5. Vaults with no recorded schemaVersion...'",
        "The requirements span three distinct concerns: (1) persisting version metadata (R1, R5), (2) detecting drift (R2), (3) reconciling templates (R3, R4).",
        "No grouping headers organize requirements by theme, making it harder for readers to understand dependencies and priorities at a glance."
      ],
      "clarification": "Flat requirements lists covering multiple themes hinder comprehension. Grouping by logical theme (Persistence, Detection, Reconciliation, Fallback) would make the requirement structure clearer.",
      "autofix_class": "auto",
      "suggested_fix": "Regroup Requirements Trace section with theme headers and original R# IDs preserved:\n\n## Requirements Trace\n\n### Persistence & Metadata\n- R1. Every Cairn vault records the schema version it was installed or last-synced against.\n- R5. Vaults with no recorded schemaVersion (pre-versioning installs) are handled gracefully — treated as \"version unknown\" and offered an opt-in sync that treats the current file as user-edited with an empty base.\n\n### Detection & Diagnostics\n- R2. cairn doctor detects drift between the vault's recorded schemaVersion and the plugin's current SCHEMA_VERSION and reports the gap actionably.\n\n### Reconciliation\n- R3. A new cairn sync-templates command reconciles the vault's CAIRN.md against the newer template without destroying user edits. A backup is written before any mutation. On successful apply, the vault's recorded schemaVersion is advanced.\n- R4. Historical template versions are bundled with the plugin so a 3-way merge always has a base revision to diff against."
    }
  ]
}

Additionally, if the user's editor has unsaved changes when sync-templates reads 'ours', the merge operates on the disk version and silently clobbers the editor version on save. There is no mtime check, no file-lock, no 'are you sure your editor is closed' warning.",
      "recommendation": "At minimum: re-stat CAIRN.md immediately before the final write; if mtime changed since the initial read, abort with a message telling the user their file changed during the operation. Better: read CAIRN.md twice — once for merge input, once immediately before write for mtime comparison — and write the backup from the second read so the backup always reflects the state being overwritten. Document the constraint: 'close your editor before running sync-templates'.",
      "location": "Key Technical Decisions — Backup before mutation; Approach step 6-8"
    },
    {
      "title": "No mutex against concurrent sync-templates invocations",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.78,
      "technique": "assumption_surfacing",
      "evidence_quote": "Flow: 1. Resolve vault path; verify it is a Cairn vault (`checkVaultState`). 2. Read `state.json`. Extract `schemaVersion` (may be undefined). ... 6. Backup vault's current `CAIRN.md` to `CAIRN.md.bak.<ISO-timestamp>` before writing.",
      "problem": "The flow is a sequence of unsynchronized reads and writes with no locking. If two sync-templates processes start simultaneously (user double-clicks a wrapper, CI runs it alongside an IDE task, a shell script pipes it twice), the interleaving can produce: (a) both read the same 'ours', both merge, both backup with different timestamps, the second write clobbers the first's merge result; (b) one writes state.json advanced to current while the other is still mid-merge using the pre-advance ours, then the second re-writes CAIRN.md with stale content; (c) doctor runs between backup and write and reports drift that no longer exists. The ISO-timestamp backup naming is specifically called out as preventing clobber of backups — but does nothing to prevent clobber of CAIRN.md itself. This is especially risky given the plan's mention of 'pipe runs' via --yes in non-interactive contexts.",
      "recommendation": "Acquire an exclusive flock on .cairn/state.json (or a dedicated .cairn/sync.lock) at command entry; release on exit. Fail fast with a clear message if the lock is held. This is a ~5-line addition that eliminates a whole class of race conditions.",
      "location": "Unit 4 Approach; Risks table"
    },
    {
      "title": "Maintainer-forgets-to-archive risk is under-mitigated for a load-bearing invariant",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.80,
      "technique": "decision_stress_testing",
      "evidence_quote": "Maintainers forget to archive the previous `templates/CAIRN.md` before bumping `SCHEMA_VERSION` | Add the bump checklist to `templates/versions/README.md`. Consider a future CI check that verifies every integer from `1..SCHEMA_VERSION` has a corresponding `CAIRN-v{N}.md` — out of scope for this plan but noted.",
      "problem": "The entire 3-way-merge capability for everyone upgrading past a given version depends on this archive existing. If a maintainer forgets even once, every user on that intermediate version is forced into the degraded 2-way path — which per the plan means 'user accepts the shipped version wholesale', destroying their edits. The mitigation is a README note and a CI check marked 'out of scope for this plan'. Given this is a load-bearing maintenance invariant with catastrophic user-visible consequences (silent edit loss via forced 2-way overwrite), deferring the CI check is a decision-scope mismatch: lightweight safety measure, heavyweight consequence. Worse: there is a second missing invariant not mentioned — that the archived CAIRN-v{N}.md is byte-equivalent to whatever was shipped as templates/CAIRN.md when SCHEMA_VERSION was N. A maintainer who bumps the version but archives a later-edited copy will have merge 'base' revisions that don't match any real user's install, producing phantom conflicts for every user.",
      "recommendation": "Move the CI check into scope for this plan. It is trivial (glob templates/versions/CAIRN-v*.md, verify 1..SCHEMA_VERSION coverage). Also add a git-tag-based assertion: on every release that bumps SCHEMA_VERSION, CI verifies that templates/versions/CAIRN-v{previous}.md is byte-identical to templates/CAIRN.md at the previous release tag. Without this second check, the archive is only nominally trustworthy.",
      "location": "Risks table, row 2; Key Technical Decisions — Bumping SCHEMA_VERSION is a deliberate release gesture"
    },
    {
      "title": "Degraded 2-way path conflates 'unknown base' with 'accept shipped wholesale', silently losing user edits",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.83,
      "technique": "premise_challenging",
      "evidence_quote": "Degraded 2-way path: if no archive exists or `schemaVersion` is unknown, present the shipped template as a plain diff against current; prompt overwrite. Treat confirmation as \"user accepts the shipped version wholesale\"; set `schemaVersion` on apply.",
      "problem": "The fallback path destroys user edits on confirm. For a pre-versioning vault (R5, explicitly in-scope), this is the common case — and the plan's earlier framing contradicts itself: 'Vaults with no recorded schemaVersion ... are handled gracefully — treated as version unknown and offered an opt-in sync that treats the current file as user-edited with an empty base.' 'Empty base' in 3-way-merge semantics means 'anything in ours OR theirs is kept' — that preserves user edits. 'Accept shipped wholesale' in the degraded path destroys them. These are opposite behaviors described for the same scenario. Which is correct? If the design is truly 'wholesale overwrite', the plan's R5 claim of 'handled gracefully' is misleading — users with hand-edited pre-versioning CAIRN.md files (likely many, per the origin note about the repo owner's own stale vault) will lose their customizations on confirm. A diff preview doesn't rescue this; real-world users routinely confirm diffs they didn't fully read.",
      "recommendation": "Resolve the contradiction explicitly. The 'empty base' 3-way approach (running git merge-file with /dev/null or an empty file as base) is the right default — it preserves non-overlapping user additions and surfaces real conflicts, matching the documented intent of R5. Reserve wholesale-overwrite for an explicit --overwrite flag. Update the decision matrix and requirements trace to match.",
      "location": "Unit 4 Approach step 9; Open Questions (Resolved) vs Requirements R5"
    },
    {
      "title": "The 'pin mtime comparison' no-op-suppression for idempotent re-apply may hide real drift",
      "severity": "low",
      "confidence": 0.68,
      "technique": "decision_stress_testing",
      "evidence_quote": "idempotent against already-merged content (`git merge-file` on identical files is a no-op, but the backup churn is real). Mitigation: check for byte-equivalence before write to suppress the backup on no-ops.",
      "problem": "Byte-equivalence suppression conflates two different success states. If a user hand-edited their CAIRN.md to exactly match the shipped template (valid outcome after manual reconciliation) and then runs sync-templates, the byte-equivalence check short-circuits and... does what? Does it still advance schemaVersion? If yes, it does so without confirmation from the user, possibly against their wishes (they may have intentionally pinned an older schema). If no, the drift warning in doctor persists forever despite the file being up to date. Neither behavior is specified.",
      "recommendation": "Specify: when the merge output is byte-equivalent to the current CAIRN.md, treat it as a clean no-op APPLY — advance schemaVersion, skip backup, print 'already current by content' — and require no user confirmation (there is no data-loss risk). Distinguish this from the 'already current by recorded version' early-exit.",
      "location": "System-Wide Impact — State lifecycle risks"

Evaluate this plan for feasibility: architecture conflicts, dependency gaps, migration risks, implementability. Check against the real codebase.

Document type: plan
Document path: docs/plans/2026-04-17-002-feat-template-schema-migration-plan.md

Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/cairn. Relevant files referenced in plan: src/lib/constants.ts, src/lib/vault.ts, src/lib/templates.ts, src/commands/init.ts, src/commands/doctor.ts, src/cli.ts, tests/init.test.ts, templates/CAIRN.md.

Return ONLY valid JSON per the findings schema. Evidence quotes required. Set finding_type and autofix_class. Use Grep/Glob/Read to verify claims against actual code.

Read the plan file directly.

content.text:
Now I have enough context. Let me synthesize the findings.

The product context is clear: Cairn is a **hybrid** product — a developer CLI/plugin shipped externally (users run `bunx cairn ...`) but operating on a *personal* knowledge vault that is read by agents. Adoption is earned. The codebase is tiny (~956 LOC total in `src/`), maintained by a small team (effectively one person — "the current repo owner's `~/cairn/CAIRN.md`"). Maintenance surface, cognitive load, and trajectory weigh heavily.

Key tensions I see:

1. **Evidence base is N=1.** The entire "live gap" premise rests on the repo owner's own vault being stale. That is a sample of one, not a user complaint, metric, or incident.

2. **Prose schema vs. merge semantics mismatch.** `templates/CAIRN.md` is 392 lines of human-readable prose that the agent reads. `git merge-file` performs line-based textual 3-way merge with no awareness of markdown sections. A conflict-marker-laden CAIRN.md becomes an agent-operated-on file — the agent might read `<<<<<<<` markers as content. This risk is acknowledged only obliquely.

3. **Maintainer checklist as substrate.** The correctness of 3-way merge depends on humans remembering to copy the previous `CAIRN.md` to `templates/versions/CAIRN-v{N}.md` before bumping. The plan names this risk but defers the CI check. For a tiny-team project, this is where discipline erodes.

4. **Simpler alternative exists.** Since the schema is prose and the target user is an agent, the lowest-cost reconciliation is: on drift, print a diff and tell the user to manually update — OR simply re-run `init --force-template` which rewrites `CAIRN.md` wholesale (after backup). The plan itself deprecates this ("2-way forces hand-reconciliation") without quantifying how often users actually edit CAIRN.md. If they rarely edit it (plausible — it's agent-instruction boilerplate), then the 3-way infrastructure is overkill.

5. **Opportunity cost.** The ideation ranked this #1 at 90% confidence, but ideas #5 (persistent findings substrate, 85%), #6 (open-threads protocol, 85%, Low complexity), #7 (auto-curated context.md, 75%) have higher or equal compounding value with equal-or-lower complexity. In particular, idea #6 ("dead metadata sitting right there") is explicitly called out as "smallest-footprint, highest-signal" and is being deferred.

6. **Trajectory concern.** Introducing `SCHEMA_VERSION` + archive directory + sync command + degraded 2-way path + decision matrix + backup convention is a significant permanent surface area increase on a 956-LOC codebase. Every future schema bump now requires the maintainer checklist + archived version file + release note + CHANGELOG entry. This raises the cost of schema evolution — the opposite of the stated goal.

7. **Identity impact (unexamined).** The product pitches itself as "pure markdown ⇒ vast tooling ecosystem (git, grep, Obsidian)." Adding a git-merge-file dependency, conflict markers in vault files, and a `templates/versions/` archive starts to say "Cairn is a versioned CLI tool with a migration framework" — that's a different identity from "persistent markdown knowledge vault." Not necessarily wrong, but the plan doesn't acknowledge the bet.

8. **Inversion: what makes this fail?** The plan ships, users run `sync-templates`, get conflict markers in CAIRN.md, don't know git conflict syntax, leave markers in the file, agent reads markers as content, vault behavior degrades. The "print a clear hint" mitigation assumes users read CLI output carefully.

9. **Goal-work alignment — R3.** R3 says "reconciles the vault's CAIRN.md against the newer template without destroying user edits." But the degraded 2-way path explicitly says "Treat confirmation as user accepts the shipped version wholesale" — that *does* destroy user edits when no archive exists. This is surfaced as acceptable, but it is in tension with the stated requirement.

10. **Unserved goal.** The ideation lists SessionStart drift nudge as part of idea #1, but the plan defers it. Without the nudge, the whole value chain depends on users running `cairn doctor` regularly. What evidence exists that they do?

```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "premise-evidence-n-of-1",
      "category": "premise-challenge",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "title": "Entire premise rests on a single user's vault (the author's own)",
      "evidence_quote": "Confirmed live gap: the current repo owner's `~/cairn/CAIRN.md` is already older than `templates/CAIRN.md` in the repo (verified today via diff). That divergence surfaced organically; a migration path would have caught it automatically.",
      "analysis": "The sole concrete evidence for 'template drift is a problem worth solving now' is that the plan's author has a stale CAIRN.md in their own vault. There are no referenced user complaints, GitHub issues, install telemetry, or data from other vaults. The ideation document (idea #1, origin) makes the same N=1 appeal: 'User's own ~/cairn/CAIRN.md is already stale vs current repo template — this is a live gap, not hypothetical.' But the author noticing drift in their own dogfood install is not evidence that drift affects adopters or that it causes real problems in agent behavior. The plan never quantifies how agents degrade when CAIRN.md is one version behind — the assumption that a stale template breaks anything is itself untested. For an external plugin with a small user base, 'I noticed it in my own vault' is a hypothesis to validate, not a validated problem.",
      "recommendation": "Before building the versioning/archive/merge substrate, confirm the problem's scope and severity. (1) Check what actually breaks when CAIRN.md is stale — are v0.3.0-era vaults observably worse at following v0.5.0 conventions? If not, drift may be cosmetic. (2) Ask users (or check issues/Discord/feedback) whether they have hit this.       "analysis": "CAIRN.md is agent-instruction prose, not user-authored content. The plan acknowledges this: the schema is 'still prose for the agent to follow.' How often do users meaningfully edit CAIRN.md after init? The plan doesn't say. If the answer is 'rarely' (plausible — it's boilerplate that defines how the agent interacts with the vault), then the entire 3-way merge apparatus is over-engineered. A dramatically simpler path: `cairn sync-templates` writes a backup (`CAIRN.md.bak.<ts>`), shows a diff, prompts for confirmation, and on yes overwrites CAIRN.md wholesale and updates schemaVersion. This costs roughly one command file, no archive directory, no git-merge-file dependency, no degraded path, no conflict-marker UX problem, no maintainer checklist for archiving previous versions. Users who customized their CAIRN.md still have the backup and can re-apply their edits. The plan's own 'degraded 2-way' path is exactly this — the plan already admits this mode is acceptable when no archive exists. Why not start there for all cases? Build the 3-way archive only if/when you observe users actually losing customizations they care about.",
      "recommendation": "Strip Unit 2 (archive) and the 3-way merge path from Unit 4. Ship: schemaVersion recorded at init (Unit 1), doctor reports drift (Unit 3), sync-templates does backup + diff + prompt + overwrite + version bump. Total complexity drops materially, maintenance surface shrinks, git-merge-file dependency goes away, conflict-marker UX risk disappears. If future telemetry or feedback shows users routinely customize CAIRN.md and lose work on sync, *then* build the 3-way archive. This is a textbook 'defer until the third occurrence' moment — you have one author-reported instance of drift, zero reports of edit loss."
    },
    {
      "id": "agent-readable-conflict-markers",

We do not build an interactive conflict resolver in the CLI — `git merge-file`'s output is the standard artifact, and editors already handle it.",
      "analysis": "CAIRN.md is not a normal source file — it is loaded and treated as authoritative instructions by the Claude Code agent on every session. If `git merge-file` leaves `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>` markers in the file and the user does not immediately resolve them (they close their terminal, forget, run another session), the agent then reads a CAIRN.md with corrupt markdown structure and two competing versions of a workflow. The mitigation is 'print a clear hint on exit 1,' which depends entirely on the user reading and acting on the CLI hint. There is no guard that prevents the agent from operating on a conflicted CAIRN.md — no '.cairn.conflicted' flag, no refusal in inject hook, no doctor check for conflict markers. This is the top failure scenario: the plan ships as written, a user runs sync, gets conflicts, closes the terminal intending to come back, and the next session proceeds with a broken contract file. The 'skeptical memory' rule in CAIRN.md actually makes this worse — the agent is instructed to trust this file.",
      "recommendation": "If the 3-way path survives, add a safety: after writing conflicted output, mark the vault state (e.g., `state.json: { schemaConflict: true }`) so the inject hook and doctor both refuse to proceed or loudly warn until markers are resolved. Better: sidestep the issue by writing the conflicted output to `CAIRN.md.merge-pending` rather than CAIRN.md itself, leaving the original file intact and instructing the user to resolve and rename. This also decouples 'agent keeps working' from 'user hasn't finished reconciling.'"
    },
    {
      "id": "prose-schema-vs-line-merge",
      "category": "premise-challenge",

      "evidence_quote": "**3-way merge over diff-and-prompt.** Showing the user a two-way diff between their file and the new template forces them to hand-reconcile even when the changes don't conflict. A 3-way merge auto-resolves non-conflicting hunks and only surfaces real conflicts. `git merge-file` does this in one call.",
      "analysis": "`git merge-file` operates on lines of text with no awareness of markdown structure. CAIRN.md is 392 lines of structured prose with tables, code blocks, and workflow steps where ordering and cross-references matter. A 'non-conflicting' merge that splices a v2 paragraph into a section while keeping a user-edited related section can produce a coherent-looking CAIRN.md that now has two contradictory instructions for the same workflow — exactly the failure mode listed in the Risks table ('schema bump where conflict-free merge produces a semantically broken vault'). The plan explicitly declares this out of scope ('a release-quality concern for whoever bumps SCHEMA_VERSION'). But the 3-way merge was chosen specifically to 'auto-resolve non-conflicting hunks' — the very step most likely to produce these invisible breakages. The case for 3-way merge over overwrite+backup weakens once you acknowledge the auto-merge can silently produce bad prose.",
      "recommendation": "Either (a) fall back to overwrite + backup (see finding simpler-alt-overwrite-with-backup), which sidesteps this entirely, or (b) add a required post-merge review step where sync-templates always shows the final merged file to the user and requires explicit confirmation, regardless of whether conflicts were detected — making the 'auto-resolve' benefit less automatic but safer."

Idea #5 (persistent findings substrate, 85%, 'Highest-leverage compounding win across the candidate set'). Idea #7 (auto-curated context.md). Those ideas build user-visible, compounding behavior — every session gets richer. This plan builds plumbing whose value only materializes the next time SCHEMA_VERSION bumps and only if the user notices drift enough to run sync-templates. For an external product earning adoption, shipping a 'your template is N versions behind' warning is less compelling than shipping 'your vault now surfaces unresolved threads from prior sessions.' The plan does not compare itself against these alternatives or explain why migration infrastructure precedes them.",
      "recommendation": "Make the sequencing explicit: either argue why migration infrastructure must precede user-visible compounding features (e.g., 'without versioning, any feature we ship in CAIRN.md drifts'), or reorder — ship idea #6 next and treat schema versioning as the minimum viable observability (Unit 1 + Unit 3 only) so the heavy infrastructure doesn't block higher-leverage work."

The product context is clear: Cairn is a **hybrid** product — a developer CLI/plugin shipped externally (users run `bunx cairn ...`) but operating on a *personal* knowledge vault that is read by agents. Adoption is earned. The codebase is tiny (~956 LOC total in `src/`), maintained by a small team (effectively one person — "the current repo owner's `~/cairn/CAIRN.md`"). Maintenance surface, cognitive load, and trajectory weigh heavily.

Key tensions I see:

1. **Evidence base is N=1.** The entire "live gap" premise rests on the repo owner's own vault being stale. That is a sample of one, not a user complaint, metric, or incident.

2. **Prose schema vs. merge semantics mismatch.** `templates/CAIRN.md` is 392 lines of human-readable prose that the agent reads. `git merge-file` performs line-based textual 3-way merge with no awareness of markdown sections. A conflict-marker-laden CAIRN.md becomes an agent-operated-on file — the agent might read `<<<<<<<` markers as content. This risk is acknowledged only obliquely.

3. **Maintainer checklist as substrate.** The correctness of 3-way merge depends on humans remembering to copy the previous `CAIRN.md` to `templates/versions/CAIRN-v{N}.md` before bumping. The plan names this risk but defers the CI check. For a tiny-team project, this is where discipline erodes.

4. **Simpler alternative exists.** Since the schema is prose and the target user is an agent, the lowest-cost reconciliation is: on drift, print a diff and tell the user to manually update — OR simply re-run `init --force-template` which rewrites `CAIRN.md` wholesale (after backup). The plan itself deprecates this ("2-way forces hand-reconciliation") without quantifying how often users actually edit CAIRN.md. If they rarely edit it (plausible — it's agent-instruction boilerplate), then the 3-way infrastructure is overkill.

5. **Opportunity cost.** The ideation ranked this #1 at 90% confidence, but ideas #5 (persistent findings substrate, 85%), #6 (open-threads protocol, 85%, Low complexity), #7 (auto-curated context.md, 75%) have higher or equal compounding value with equal-or-lower complexity. In particular, idea #6 ("dead metadata sitting right there") is explicitly called out as "smallest-footprint, highest-signal" and is being deferred.

6. **Trajectory concern.** Introducing `SCHEMA_VERSION` + archive directory + sync command + degraded 2-way path + decision matrix + backup convention is a significant permanent surface area increase on a 956-LOC codebase. Every future schema bump now requires the maintainer checklist + archived version file + release note + CHANGELOG entry. This raises the cost of schema evolution — the opposite of the stated goal.

7. **Identity impact (unexamined).** The product pitches itself as "pure markdown ⇒ vast tooling ecosystem (git, grep, Obsidian)." Adding a git-merge-file dependency, conflict markers in vault files, and a `templates/versions/` archive starts to say "Cairn is a versioned CLI tool with a migration framework" — that's a different identity from "persistent markdown knowledge vault." Not necessarily wrong, but the plan doesn't acknowledge the bet.

8. **Inversion: what makes this fail?** The plan ships, users run `sync-templates`, get conflict markers in CAIRN.md, don't know git conflict syntax, leave markers in the file, agent reads markers as content, vault behavior degrades. The "print a clear hint" mitigation assumes users read CLI output carefully.

9. **Goal-work alignment — R3.** R3 says "reconciles the vault's CAIRN.md against the newer template without destroying user edits." But the degraded 2-way path explicitly says "Treat confirmation as user accepts the shipped version wholesale" — that *does* destroy user edits when no archive exists. This is surfaced as acceptable, but it is in tension with the stated requirement.

10. **Unserved goal.** The ideation lists SessionStart drift nudge as part of idea #1, but the plan defers it. Without the nudge, the whole value chain depends on users running `cairn doctor` regularly. What evidence exists that they do?

```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "premise-evidence-n-of-1",
      "category": "premise-challenge",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "title": "Entire premise rests on a single user's vault (the author's own)",
      "evidence_quote": "Confirmed live gap: the current repo owner's `~/cairn/CAIRN.md` is already older than `templates/CAIRN.md` in the repo (verified today via diff). That divergence surfaced organically; a migration path would have caught it automatically.",
      "analysis": "The sole concrete evidence for 'template drift is a problem worth solving now' is that the plan's author has a stale CAIRN.md in their own vault. There are no referenced user complaints, GitHub issues, install telemetry, or data from other vaults. The ideation document (idea #1, origin) makes the same N=1 appeal: 'User's own ~/cairn/CAIRN.md is already stale vs current repo template — this is a live gap, not hypothetical.' But the author noticing drift in their own dogfood install is not evidence that drift affects adopters or that it causes real problems in agent behavior. The plan never quantifies how agents degrade when CAIRN.md is one version behind — the assumption that a stale template breaks anything is itself untested. For an external plugin with a small user base, 'I noticed it in my own vault' is a hypothesis to validate, not a validated problem.",
      "recommendation": "Before building the versioning/archive/merge substrate, confirm the problem's scope and severity. (1) Check what actually breaks when CAIRN.md is stale — are v0.3.0-era vaults observably worse at following v0.5.0 conventions? If not, drift may be cosmetic. (2) Ask users (or check issues/Discord/feedback) whether they have hit this. (3) Consider shipping only Unit 1 (record schemaVersion at init) + Unit 3 (doctor reports drift with a one-line remediation hint) and stop there. That lands the observability with ~30 LOC; the 3-way merge, archive directory, and sync command can wait for evidence of user demand."
    },
    {
      "id": "simpler-alt-overwrite-with-backup",

If the answer is 'rarely' (plausible — it's boilerplate that defines how the agent interacts with the vault), then the entire 3-way merge apparatus is over-engineered. A dramatically simpler path: `cairn sync-templates` writes a backup (`CAIRN.md.bak.<ts>`), shows a diff, prompts for confirmation, and on yes overwrites CAIRN.md wholesale and updates schemaVersion. This costs roughly one command file, no archive directory, no git-merge-file dependency, no degraded path, no conflict-marker UX problem, no maintainer checklist for archiving previous versions. Users who customized their CAIRN.md still have the backup and can re-apply their edits. The plan's own 'degraded 2-way' path is exactly this — the plan already admits this mode is acceptable when no archive exists. Why not start there for all cases? Build the 3-way archive only if/when you observe users actually losing customizations they care about.",

Ship: schemaVersion recorded at init (Unit 1), doctor reports drift (Unit 3), sync-templates does backup + diff + prompt + overwrite + version bump. Total complexity drops materially, maintenance surface shrinks, git-merge-file dependency goes away, conflict-marker UX risk disappears. If future telemetry or feedback shows users routinely customize CAIRN.md and lose work on sync, *then* build the 3-way archive. This is a textbook 'defer until the third occurrence' moment — you have one author-reported instance of drift, zero reports of edit loss."

      "id": "agent-readable-conflict-markers",

We do not build an interactive conflict resolver in the CLI — `git merge-file`'s output is the standard artifact, and editors already handle it.",

If `git merge-file` leaves `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>` markers in the file and the user does not immediately resolve them (they close their terminal, forget, run another session), the agent then reads a CAIRN.md with corrupt markdown structure and two competing versions of a workflow. The mitigation is 'print a clear hint on exit 1,' which depends entirely on the user reading and acting on the CLI hint. There is no guard that prevents the agent from operating on a conflicted CAIRN.md — no '.cairn.conflicted' flag, no refusal in inject hook, no doctor check for conflict markers. This is the top failure scenario: the plan ships as written, a user runs sync, gets conflicts, closes the terminal intending to come back, and the next session proceeds with a broken contract file. The 'skeptical memory' rule in CAIRN.md actually makes this worse — the agent is instructed to trust this file.",

Better: sidestep the issue by writing the conflicted output to `CAIRN.md.merge-pending` rather than CAIRN.md itself, leaving the original file intact and instructing the user to resolve and rename. This also decouples 'agent keeps working' from 'user hasn't finished reconciling.'"

A 'non-conflicting' merge that splices a v2 paragraph into a section while keeping a user-edited related section can produce a coherent-looking CAIRN.md that now has two contradictory instructions for the same workflow — exactly the failure mode listed in the Risks table ('schema bump where conflict-free merge produces a semantically broken vault'). The plan explicitly declares this out of scope ('a release-quality concern for whoever bumps SCHEMA_VERSION'). But the 3-way merge was chosen specifically to 'auto-resolve non-conflicting hunks' — the very step most likely to produce these invisible breakages. The case for 3-way merge over overwrite+backup weakens once you acknowledge the auto-merge can silently produce bad prose.",
      "recommendation": "Either (a) fall back to overwrite + backup (see finding simpler-alt-overwrite-with-backup), which sidesteps this entirely, or (b) add a required post-merge review step where sync-templates always shows the final merged file to the user and requires explicit confirmation, regardless of whether conflicts were detected — making the 'auto-resolve' benefit less automatic but safer."

Idea #6 (open-threads protocol, 85% confidence, Low complexity) is explicitly described as 'Dead metadata sitting right there. Smallest-footprint, highest-signal win in the set.' Idea #5 (persistent findings substrate, 85%, 'Highest-leverage compounding win across the candidate set'). Idea #7 (auto-curated context.md). Those ideas build user-visible, compounding behavior — every session gets richer. This plan builds plumbing whose value only materializes the next time SCHEMA_VERSION bumps and only if the user notices drift enough to run sync-templates. For an external product earning adoption, shipping a 'your template is N versions behind' warning is less compelling than shipping 'your vault now surfaces unresolved threads from prior sessions.' The plan does not compare itself against these alternatives or explain why migration infrastructure precedes them.",
      "recommendation": "Make the sequencing explicit: either argue why migration infrastructure must precede user-visible compounding features (e.g., 'without versioning, any feature we ship in CAIRN.md drifts'), or reorder — ship idea #6 next and treat schema versioning as the minimum viable observability (Unit 1 + Unit 3 only) so the heavy infrastructure doesn't block higher-leverage work."

      "severity": "moderate",
      "confidence": 0.70,

      "evidence_quote": "**Bumping `SCHEMA_VERSION` is a deliberate release gesture.** When maintainers change `templates/CAIRN.md` in a way they consider a new schema version, they (a) bump `SCHEMA_VERSION` in `src/lib/constants.ts`, (b) copy the previous `templates/CAIRN.md` into `templates/versions/CAIRN-v{previous}.md`. This is a two-step checklist item, documented in a CONTRIBUTING note.",
      "analysis": "The full src/ tree is roughly 956 LOC. This plan adds a new command file, a new archive directory that grows monotonically forever, a maintainer checklist ('did you remember to copy the old template?'), a CI check deferred as future work, a new degraded-mode path, a decision matrix with 6 states, and a new concept (schemaVersion distinct from version) that every doctor/init/sync caller must keep coherent. The 'Risk: Maintainers forget to archive the previous templates/CAIRN.md before bumping SCHEMA_VERSION' bullet acknowledges the ritual is fragile. For internal/hybrid tools maintained by one or two people, every checklist item is a latent bug — a future release will forget, and a user running sync-templates will hit the degraded path silently. The plan also introduces a decoupling ('plugin VERSION vs SCHEMA_VERSION') that creates a new coherence question on every release: did the template actually change in a schema-meaningful way, or not? The answer is now a deliberate human judgment each release.",

Write it as part of this plan's scope. Alternatively, eliminate the decoupling: treat every release that touches templates/CAIRN.md as a schema bump, auto-archived by a pre-release script, removing the 'did I remember?' question entirely."

      "id": "r3-contradicts-degraded-path",

      "analysis": "R3 promises reconciliation 'without destroying user edits.' But the degraded 2-way path — which fires when no archive exists or the vault has no recorded schemaVersion (precisely the cases most likely to have customized files, since they are older vaults) — explicitly treats user confirmation as 'user accepts the shipped version wholesale.' That is destroying edits by definition (the backup preserves them on disk, but the live CAIRN.md now has none of them). The requirement should either be narrowed ('without silently destroying edits; prompts before any overwrite; backup always written') or the degraded path needs to do better (e.g., always attempt a diff-assisted hand-merge). As written, R5's graceful handling of pre-versioning vaults ships a contradiction with R3.",

That means the user must (a) know doctor exists, (b) remember to run it, (c) read the warning lines, (d) act on the remediation hint. The ideation explicitly called out SessionStart nudge as part of idea #1 — the thing that would actually make drift visible during normal use — and this plan defers it. The result: schema drift continues to be invisible to users who don't proactively run doctor, even after the sync command ships. The 'premature nudging before the fix path is smooth' reasoning is coherent, but it means the first release of this feature will have limited observable impact. If the goal is to catch drift, the nudge is the catcher; without it, sync-templates is a tool nobody reaches for.",

The latter framing requires the nudge to be real."
